questions.question: Define Software Project Management and list its primary objectives.
keyPoints: SPM is the discipline of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling software development projects
Objectives: Complete project within time, cost, quality constraints
Meet stakeholder requirements and manage changes effectively
Optimize resource allocation and ensure successful delivery

questions.question: Explain the purpose and components of a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).
keyPoints: WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of total project scope
Components: Project phases, deliverables, work packages
Work packages are the smallest units in WBS
Each element should be measurable and have assigned responsible person

questions.question: Describe the risk management process in software projects.
keyPoints: Risk Identification: Brainstorming, checklists, expert interviews
Risk Analysis: Qualitative (probability-impact matrix) and Quantitative (Monte Carlo)
Risk Response Planning: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept
Risk Monitoring: Continuous tracking and updating risk register

questions.question: Explain the difference between Quality Assurance and Quality Control in software projects.
keyPoints: QA is process-oriented and proactive - prevents defects through process improvement
QC is product-oriented and reactive - detects defects through testing and inspection
QA focuses on processes, QC focuses on products
Both are essential for quality software delivery

questions.question: List and explain the activities performed during project closure phase.
keyPoints: Deliver final product and obtain acceptance
Conduct post-project review and document lessons learned
Release project resources (team, equipment, budget)
Archive project documents and update organizational assets
Formally close all contracts and notify stakeholders

questions.question: Explain the Critical Path Method (CPM) and its significance in project scheduling.
keyPoints: CPM identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities (critical path)
Critical path determines minimum project completion time
Activities on critical path have zero float/slack
Any delay in critical path tasks delays entire project
CPM helps in resource optimization and schedule compression

questions.question: What is Earned Value Management (EVM)? Explain any two key EVM metrics.
keyPoints: EVM integrates scope, schedule, and cost measurements
Planned Value (PV): Budgeted cost for scheduled work
Earned Value (EV): Budgeted cost for completed work
Actual Cost (AC): Actual cost of completed work
Schedule Variance (SV = EV - PV) and Cost Variance (CV = EV - AC)

questions.question: Describe the project planning process in Software Project Management. Include details on project scheduling, resource allocation, and cost estimation techniques.
keyPoints: Project Planning involves defining objectives and creating action plans
Project Scheduling: Define activities, sequence them, estimate durations using PERT/CPM, create Gantt chart
Resource Allocation: Identify required resources, assign to tasks, perform resource leveling
Cost Estimation: Use techniques like expert judgment, analogy, parametric models, bottom-up, three-point estimation
Planning creates baseline for monitoring and control
All activities interrelate - schedule affects cost, resource allocation affects both

questions.question: Explain the project monitoring and control activities. How do these activities help in successful project delivery?
keyPoints: Monitoring: Track progress, measure performance metrics, compare against baseline
Control: Analyze variances, identify causes, take corrective/preventive actions
Key areas: Scope control, schedule control, cost control, quality control, risk monitoring
Tools: Earned Value Analysis, status reports, dashboards, variance reports
Benefits: Early detection of issues, informed decision-making, stakeholder confidence
Enables proactive management rather than reactive problem-solving
Ensures project stays on track for successful delivery

questions.question: Discuss the change management process in software projects. Why is it important to control changes?
keyPoints: Change Management Process: Submit change request, evaluate impact (schedule, cost, scope), approve/reject, implement, verify
Change Control Board (CCB) reviews and approves changes
Importance of controlling changes: Prevents scope creep, maintains project baseline
Uncontrolled changes lead to schedule delays, cost overruns, quality issues
Change management ensures all changes are properly documented and assessed
Balances flexibility with project stability
Stakeholder satisfaction through controlled enhancement implementation

questions.question: Explain the various cost estimation techniques used in software projects. Compare at least three techniques with their advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Expert Judgment: Based on experienced estimates - quick but subjective
Analogy Estimation: Uses historical data from similar projects - accurate if projects are truly similar
Parametric Modeling: Uses statistical relationships - good for standardized projects
Bottom-up: Estimate each work package - accurate but time-consuming
Three-point Estimation: Uses optimistic, pessimistic, most likely values - accounts for uncertainty
Comparison: Accuracy vs time/cost trade-off, project complexity consideration, data availability
